text: The surgeon makes an incision on the peritoneum just above the deep ring in a case of indirect inguinal hernia.

The surgeon uses electrocautery to divide the layers of the transversalis fascia, creating a preperitoneal or extraperitoneal space. A green line on the right side of the screen indicates the use of an energy source for tissue division.

The surgeon uses a precise energy source to maintain the dissection plane, creating lateral and medial spaces before addressing the central zone. The dissection reveals the indirect hernia sac extending towards the scrotum through the deep ring, with the arrow indicating the inferior epigastric vessels at the twelve o'clock position on the screen.

The surgeon performs medial dissection in the Cooper's ligament area, exposing the pubic region from within. Sharp dissection with short bursts of cautery is used to maintain clear planes and minimize blood loss. Anatomical landmarks, including the lateral pelvic wall with fascia and fat covering the vessels, are visible.

The surgeon liberates the hernia sac from the surrounding cord structures, including the vas deferens, pampiniform plexus, and gonadal vessels. Dissection is performed midway between the sac's entry into the deep ring and the peritoneal opening, ensuring the sac is completely exposed. The distal part of the sac is left intact, and the dissection continues towards the peritoneal reflection where the hernia defect begins.

The surgeon uses electrocautery with minimal charring to dissect fibro fatty tissue around the vasculature, while providing traction with the left hand. The external iliac vein is visible medially. The dissection focuses on the lateral pelvic wall and Cooper's ligament, ensuring complete fascia removal to prevent mesh recurrence. Femoral nodes are visible in the femoral canal but are left undisturbed.

The surgeon performs dissection above the obturator foramen, focusing on the lateral compartment. The patient has a significant amount of fat, which aids in preventing nerve injuries. The dissection ensures a minimum distance of seven to eight centimeters from the deep ring to the peritoneal reflection to prevent mesh folding.

The surgeon performs a lower incision using the robotic platform, creating a superior flap and dissecting in the medial compartment. Small branches to the rectus abdominis are pointed out. A 3D Max large mesh by Bard BD is placed into the groin, fitting nicely. The surgeon performs additional dissection in the triangle of doom, defined by the vas deferens medially, gonadal vessels laterally, and reflected peritoneum as the base, with iliac vessels as contents. The mesh sits beautifully in the lateral angle, promoting scar plate formation.

The surgeon shapes and places a mesh in the groin area, ensuring medial overlap for proper fit. The mesh is evaluated for potential displacement by the peritoneum once the patient begins walking. Sutures are used for fixation and closure of the peritoneum. The surgeon performs sharp dissection to clear fibro fatty tissue in the extraperitoneal space, revealing the transversalis fascia and its two layers.

The surgeon uses a robotic platform to strip the transversalis fascia from the peritoneum, leaving tissue towards the groin to protect nerves. The deep ring aspect is cleared, revealing a small indirect hernia. Fat is gradually cleared from the lateral pelvic wall, creating horizontal space for the procedure. Medial attachments are shown, with further clearance needed before meeting the mesh on the opposite side.

The surgeon carefully dissects the fibro fatty tissue at the Cooper's ligament area, removing a sliding fascia. A small accessory obturator vessel is encountered but does not hinder the dissection. The gonadal vessels are visible as the surgeon performs precise cautery dissection around them, ensuring preservation of the vas and preventing injury.

The surgeon demonstrates anatomical landmarks and prepares the surgical bed for mesh placement, creating a superior flap using robotic articulation. The transversalis fascia is divided to form a flap, and compartments are connected medially to reinforce the groin. The surgeon enters the opposite side groin, connecting spaces to ensure the mesh sits securely. A Bard 3D Max large soft mesh is placed effortlessly in the left side, where the hernia is small.

The surgeon pushes the mesh into the groin, ensuring it takes the correct shape and meets the opposite side counterpart. The peritoneum is adjusted to allow the mesh to sit properly without effort. The surgeon uses a mega suture cut instrument to perform ergonomic adjustments and passes a suture through Cooper's ligament, pulling the thread to secure the mesh. Loose sutures are placed to orient the mesh correctly, focusing on lateral coverage due to bilateral indirect hernias.

The surgeon uses a three-point fixation technique for the mesh, with two fixation points medially and one laterally. A barbed suture from the company Quill is used for the closure of the peritoneum, employing a simple continuous suturing technique. The barbs are carefully buried to minimize exposure and potential adhesions, ensuring a snug closure of the peritoneum.

The surgeon repeats the suturing steps on the opposite side, employing backhand suturing technique with a Quill suture. The suture's large loop at the tail end facilitates easy feeding of the free end. The surgeon reaches the middle edge of the incision, taking a few more bites to complete the closure of the peritoneum.

The surgeon adjusts instruments and camera to optimize ergonomics while closing the hernia sac on the left side. A small opening created during flap formation is also closed. The needles are removed, marking the completion of the surgery.
